purpose: Determines whether conditions for a demand-driven wage-price spiral are present by testing three jointly necessary conditions, providing a base-rate falsification framework
components: Condition 1: Unemployment below 3.8%
Condition 2: Wage growth above 5%
Condition 3: Inflation expectations de-anchored
All three conditions must be simultaneously met (conjunctive test)
Institutional preconditions check: union density, COLA clause prevalence, Fed credibility
Validated across 8 post-1960 episodes
current_assessment: Current conditions meet 0 of 3 required conditions. The 2021-2023 natural experiment with 2 of 3 conditions produced no spiral. Residual tail probability estimated at 15-20% by one agent but partially refuted as internally inconsistent.
limitations: Historical base rates may not capture novel institutional dynamics
Threshold values are themselves uncertain and regime-dependent
Does not account for potential new transmission mechanisms (social media, gig platforms)
Conjunctive framing may miss partial-condition scenarios with non-spiral but still-elevated inflation

purpose: Identifies the correct monitoring sequence for anticipating labor market downturns, correcting the common practice of treating payroll releases as leading indicators
components: Stage 1: Credit spreads widen (HY OAS) — 3-12 months before labor deterioration
Stage 2: Equities price 60-80% of eventual drawdown
Stage 3: Labor market data confirms recession (Sahm Rule triggers)
Primary monitoring tool: HY OAS spread level and trajectory
Secondary: equity market pricing of employment-sensitive sectors
current_assessment: HY OAS at 350-420bp leans equity-side (benign), not signaling imminent labor market deterioration. This is the most important correction to conventional monitoring practice.
limitations: Based on three recession episodes (1990, 2000-01, 2007-09) — small sample
Current fiscal deficit support may alter the credit-labor transmission timing
Credit market structure has changed (passive flows, CLO demand) since calibration episodes
Does not specify precise OAS threshold levels for action

purpose: Classifies the macro regime into four quadrants (growth x inflation) using the labor market as the regime-determining variable, then maps to asset allocation and stock-bond correlation regime
components: Quadrant 1: Strong growth + low inflation (goldilocks) — pro-risk, negative stock-bond correlation
Quadrant 2: Strong growth + high inflation (overheating) — real assets, inflation protection
Quadrant 3: Weak growth + high inflation (stagflation) — defensive, positive stock-bond correlation
Quadrant 4: Weak growth + low inflation (disinflation) — duration, negative stock-bond correlation
Labor determines quadrant via three channels: consumption floor (~70% GDP), ULC → services CPI transmission, Fed dual mandate reaction function
Within-quadrant factor rotation via sectoral wage dispersion (speculative)
current_assessment: Economy positioned near the bifurcation between Quadrant 1 (soft landing) and Quadrant 3 (stagflation). Cross-asset markets are pricing elements of both simultaneously, which is internally inconsistent. Immigration reversal is the key variable pushing toward stagflation.
limitations: NAIRU uncertainty (CI: 4.3-7.3%) makes real-time quadrant identification unreliable
Labor data revision risk means the quadrant signal is lagged
Better as a regime indicator than a timing tool
Sectoral wage dispersion within-quadrant signals are uncorroborated

purpose: Identifies the current labor market regime by scoring similarity to five historical analogues, providing probability-weighted scenario analysis
components: Analogue 1: 1996-2000 (productivity-led expansion) — high similarity
Analogue 2: 2015-2019 (gradual tightening, low inflation) — high similarity
Analogue 3: 1965-69 (overheating, proto-stagflation) — moderate similarity
Analogue 4: 2005-07 (housing-led, pre-crisis) — low-moderate similarity
Analogue 5: 1973-75 (supply shock stagflation) — low similarity
Systematic similarity scoring and decomposition methodology
Base case: blended 1996-2000 / 2015-2019 at 50-60% probability benign moderation
current_assessment: Base case is a blended 1996-2000 / 2015-2019 analogue with 50-60% probability of benign moderation. Immigration reversal and fiscal dynamics are the key variables that could shift toward the 1965-69 overheating analogue.
limitations: Historical analogues never repeat exactly
Post-COVID structural shifts (remote work, AI, fiscal scale) have no historical precedent
Similarity scoring methodology is subjective
Cannot anticipate genuinely novel dynamics

purpose: Provides specific, measurable thresholds for labor market monitoring to signal regime transitions, rated as the most operationally useful output across all agents
components: JOLTS layoff rate: >1.3% signals hoarding break risk
JOLTS quits rate: >2.8% signals labor market re-tightening
Average hourly earnings: crossing above 4.5% signals overheating; crossing below 3.0% signals disinflation
Prime-age (25-54) LFPR thresholds for structural labor supply assessment
HY OAS as leading credit indicator (per credit-labor sequencing framework)
current_assessment: Current readings are between the thresholds, consistent with the base case of benign moderation. None of the hard thresholds have been triggered.
limitations: Thresholds derived from limited historical episodes
JOLTS data quality is degrading (response rates ~60% to ~30%)
Thresholds assume stable structural relationships that may shift
Hierarchy does not specify protocols when signals conflict

purpose: Explains why the labor market has not deteriorated despite 525bp of monetary tightening, identifying the fiscal channel as the dominant force sustaining corporate profits and labor demand
components: Kalecki identity: Profits = Investment + Government Deficit + Net Exports − Household Saving + Dividends
Current fiscal deficit at 6-7% GDP provides ~$1.8-2.0T of profit support
Private GDP growth stripped of fiscal is only 1.0-1.5%
Financial conditions divergence from policy intent (equity rallies, credit compression) further supports labor
Immigration supply expansion complemented fiscal demand support
current_assessment: The fiscal channel is the primary explanation for labor market resilience. Any analysis of labor market prospects must incorporate fiscal trajectory assumptions. The labor market is not defying monetary policy — it is being held up by fiscal policy.
limitations: The Kalecki identity is an accounting identity, not a causal model — causation is bidirectional
Fiscal sustainability concerns may eventually force consolidation
Does not specify which components of profit will adjust first under stress
Monetary-fiscal interaction effects are complex and regime-dependent
